content.assessment: Reforming admissions while preserving track structure leaves the fundamental reference-group and ranking architecture intact, meaning big-fish-little-pond effects and performance-orientation dynamics tied to selection itself are not altered. However, changing admissions criteria to reduce tutoring-sensitivity could alter who experiences labeling and expectancy effects, potentially shifting which students are perceived as 'deserving' of elite placement — a contested mechanism whose magnitude is uncertain and may be larger for previously stigmatized or low-SES students who gain access. The psychological effect of this proposal is likely more about redistributing who bears selection-related costs and benefits than reducing the costs themselves.
opportunities: If admissions redesign successfully shifts track composition toward previously excluded groups, expectancy-effect research suggests possible benefit for stigmatized students, though effect sizes are contested and likely smaller than early claims.
strengths: Could reduce a source of unequal labeling where tutoring-driven admission is misread as ability-driven, potentially mitigating expectancy effects that disadvantage low-SES students, though this magnitude is contested.
Does not require dismantling existing track structures, allowing continuity in students' self-concept reference frames during the transition.
threats: Divided institutional buy-in and partial coverage (church/private school exemption) could create inconsistent psychological experiences across the system, undermining any expectancy-related benefit.
Reform fatigue and stagnation risk (as seen in 2000s desegregation rules) could limit the durability of any labeling-effect improvement.
weaknesses: Leaves the underlying selection age and ranking structure untouched, so goal-orientation and performance-orientation pressures associated with high-stakes entrance exams persist.
Big-fish-little-pond dynamics remain fully in effect since track composition and hierarchy are preserved; only the sorting criterion changes.
